coredata.dc:title: Multimodal optical detection, identification and reduction of Cr(VI) with carbon dots and its PVA/polyNIPAM spheres based 2D luminescent photonic structures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rangel-Ayala, M.
Castillo-Gallardo, V.
Kumar, Y.
Pérez-Huerta, J.S.
Pacholski, Claudia
Agarwal, V.
coredata.dc:description: Multiple emissive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal acid-assisted method using citric acid and urea. Three main emission bands were observed at ∼431 nm, ∼501 nm, and ∼622 nm, corresponding to excitation wavelengths of 360 nm, 430 nm, and 550 nm, respectively. Simultaneous detection through salient discernible features of three metal ions has been achieved. Emission of CDs with Fe(III) shows reduction in intensity at 360 nm and 550 nm wavelengths and an enhancement of photoluminescence (PL) intensity at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm. Al(III) revealed turn-on PL signals, along with a redshift when excited at 360 and 430 nm, and turn-off intensity at excitation of 550 nm. Under all the excitation wavelengths, Cr(VI) exhibits PL quenching accompanied with an additional red shift. This sensor enhances the accuracy and reliability of the probe through multiple distinctive and characteristic responses for all the three ions for which the probes were selective. Apart from the optical detection at a certain wavelength, the Cr(VI) reduction to the less toxic Cr(III) through as-prepared CDs could be monitored at three different excitation wavelengths. The Fe(III) and Cr(VI) detection in solid state has also been demonstrated through 2D luminescent photonic structures formed with CDs-PVA (poly-vinyl alcohol)-polyNIPAM (poly (N-isopropylacrylamide)) composites that can possibly be used for anticounterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric optical sensor
2D luminescent photonic structures
Cr(VI) reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for (a) solvothermal synthesis of CDs and (b) assembly of PVA:CDs into polyNIPAM spheres.

PL spectra of sequential detection of the reduction process of Cr(VI) [2.5 mM] to Cr(III) with CDs at excitation wavelength of (a) 360 nm (reference divided by three), (b) 430 nm and (c) 550 nm. (d) Sensitivity of CDs (
0.83 μ g / m L
) to Cr(VI) in the range of 0.1
μ M
to 5 mM at excitation of 360 nm and (e) The linear plots of F0/F versus the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range from 0.1 to 9
μ M
for
λ 1
y
λ 2
(error bars corresponding to 3 different measurements) (f) DPC based colorimetric test for the photoreduction of Cr(VI).
(a) XPS high-resolution spectrum of Cr 2p and (b) Scheme of proposal of photoreduction mechanism of Cr (VI) to Cr(III) induced by CDs.

(a) Schematic representation of light diffraction using a 2D ordered array of polyNIPAM microspheres. (b) Substrate illuminated with white light. (c) Top view (through Scanning electron microscopy) of the composite deposited on the silicon substrate.

Emission spectra of the composite formed on the c-Silicon substrate, excitation wavelength of 254 nm in the presence of (a) Cr(VI) and (c) Fe(III), at different concentrations. Panels (b) and (d) show the percentage decrease of the PL intensity of the composite photonic structure for Cr(VI) and Fe(III), respectively. Error bars correspond to three experiments.
Comparison of obtained CDs with other fluorescent CDs used in the detection/reduction of Cr(VI).

row.entry: Solid sensor CDs/polymer composite with diffraction pattern
Imidazole, phosphoric acid and polyethylene glycol

entry.bold: Citric acid, urea and formic acid

head.ce:title: Multimodal optical detection, identification and reduction of Cr(VI) with carbon dots and its PVA/polyNIPAM spheres based 2D luminescent photonic structures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multiple emissive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal acid-assisted method using citric acid and urea. Three main emission bands were observed at ∼431 nm, ∼501 nm, and ∼622 nm, corresponding to excitation wavelengths of 360 nm, 430 nm, and 550 nm, respectively. Simultaneous detection through salient discernible features of three metal ions has been achieved. Emission of CDs with Fe(III) shows reduction in intensity at 360 nm and 550 nm wavelengths and an enhancement of photoluminescence (PL) intensity at an excitation wavelength of 430 nm. Al(III) revealed turn-on PL signals, along with a redshift when excited at 360 and 430 nm, and turn-off intensity at excitation of 550 nm. Under all the excitation wavelengths, Cr(VI) exhibits PL quenching accompanied with an additional red shift. This sensor enhances the accuracy and reliability of the probe through multiple distinctive and characteristic responses for all the three ions for which the probes were selective. Apart from the optical detection at a certain wavelength, the Cr(VI) reduction to the less toxic Cr(III) through as-prepared CDs could be monitored at three different excitation wavelengths. The Fe(III) and Cr(VI) detection in solid state has also been demonstrated through 2D luminescent photonic structures formed with CDs-PVA (poly-vinyl alcohol)-polyNIPAM (poly (N-isopropylacrylamide)) composites that can possibly be used for anticounterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals from industrial wastewater are a potential hazard due to their limited ability to undergo biodegradation. The introduction of toxic contaminants and pollutants into the human body, has emerged as a notable concern in modern times. Heavy metal ions are commonly found in aqueous form from industrial activities as textile, painting and agriculture industries [1]. The accumulation of these contaminants in organisms poses a substantial risk to human health [2]. Chromium, known for being toxic and oncogenic [3,4], exists in two stable oxidation states in natural waters: Cr (III) and Cr (VI) with the former being relatively less harmful [5]. Although, human body requires iron, an overload produces iron deposition in liver, cardiac fibrosis, depletion of important cell types and increases the risk of infections; an iron deficiency can be associated with anemia [6]. On the other hand overdose of aluminum can alter the blood content, musculoskeletal system, liver, kidney, the respiratory and nervous systems [7].

Detection of heavy metal traces is conventionally developed with elaborated techniques as inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), ICP-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), ICP-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS), X-ray fluorescence (XRF), neutron activation analysis (NAA), liquid chromatography (HPLC) techniques coupled with UV–visible detectors, gas chromatography, ion chromatography, isotope dilution, and hydride generation AAS [8]. Nanotechnology based simple, environmentally friendly and economically viable alternative sensors for metal ions detection are being investigated [9,10].

In particular, the remarkable optical properties, ease of synthesis, minimal toxicity, and great chemical stability of CDs have garnered considerable attention of the scientific community for the selective detection of heavy metals [11,12]. The photoluminescence of CDs is a notable characteristic that exhibits tunability across the ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) parts of the electromagnetic spectrum [13]. The fluorescent characteristics of these particles can be adjusted based on several factors, including particle size, initial substances used, duration of the reaction, and the physicochemical attributes of the particle surface [14]. The ability to adjust the fluorescence of colloidal CDs enables their potential integration into a diverse range of applications, such as lighting, imaging, and sensing [15]. One key application area for multicolored emission CDs is in the sensing of heavy metals. Multicolored emission CDs have been shown to facilitate the simultaneous identification of multiple metal ions through the allocation of a unique emission CD to each respective metal ion [16,17].

Several attempts to introduce CDs into polymeric matrices or hydrogels in order to obtain synergisticeffect, have been made. Z. Zhai et al. incorporate CDs with PolyNIPAM via hydrogen bonds to form a photosensitive composite [18]. T. Chen et al. propose a hybrid material of co-polymer (poly (N-isopropylacrylamide-co-acrylic acid)) and CDs which demonstrated thermo-sensitivity and photoluminescent, used as a temperature and pH sensor as well as a fluorescent probe for solvent recognition and Fe(III) ion detection [19].

In this contribution, the excitation wavelength dependent behavior of CDs reveal three main emission bands along the visible region, which serve towards the simultaneous and discriminating identification of the toxic heavy metals Cr(VI), Fe(III), and Al(III). In addition, CDs induced reduction of Cr(VI) to the less harmful Cr(III) could be monitored using PL spectroscopy and verified through DPC colorimetric test along with XPS analysis. Self-assembled 2D arrays of poly-N-isopropylacrylamid (polyNIPAM) colloids have been used for designing the diffraction pattern of light as part of composite of PVA and CDs for the fluorometry based specific ratiometric detection of Cr(VI) and Fe(III). The created diffraction pattern can possibly be used for anticounterfeiting application.

AgNO3 (99.6 %), NiCl2 (98 %), FeCl3 (97 %), CdCl2 (99.9 %) and K2Cr2O7 (99 %) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. CuCl2 (99 %), Cd(NO3)2 (99 %), Cu(NO3)2 (98 %), Cu(SO4)2 (98 %), ZnCl2 (97.2 %), PbCl2 (99 %), Ba(NO3)2 (99 %), CoCl2 (98 %), Ni(SO4)2 (98 %) and AlCl3 (99 %) were obtained from Fermont. 1, 5- diphenylcarbazide (DPC) was obtained from sigma Aldrich. H2SO4 (97.4 %) and formic acid 88 % were acquired from Fermont.

CDs were synthesized through solvothermal treatment of 2 g of citric acid and 4 g of urea in 20 mL of formic acid (FA) solvent at a temperature of 160 °C for a duration of 8 h in an autoclave (Fig. 1 a). After washing three times with ethanol according to Ref. [20] as purification process, the precipitates were dried at a temperature of 60 °C to obtain the powder, followed by its dilution in water (0.1 mg/mL) to be characterized and applied as optical sensor and reducing agent. A ratio of 1:29 of CDs solution: metal ion solution was employed in the specificity, and 1:119 in the sensitivity and reduction tests.

Poly (N-isopropylacrylamide) (polyNIPAM) microspheres were synthesized as given in the literature from C. Pacholski [21]. The polyNIPAM suspension was combined with the PVA/luminescent CDs solution and dispersed on hydrophylic silicon substrates. Prior to spin-coating, 8 μL of ethanol was also added to the previously coated solution to facilitate the self-assembly of the polyNIPAM microspheres. (Fig. 1b).

PL emission spectra of CDs and Rhodamine 6G as reference (QY = 92 % in water [22]) were recorded to determine the integrated fluorescence intensity. The integrated PL intensity was plotted against the absorbance value (absorbance was maintained less than 0.1). QY percentage was calculated using the following equation [23]:
Q Y =
Q Y
r e f
m s
m
r e f
n s 2
n
r e f
2
where
m
is the slope and
n
is the refractive index of the solvent (which is the same for the reference and sample), the subscript “ref” designates the reference solution and " s " is the sample.

The colorimetric method using 1,5-diphenyl carbazide (DPC), to detect the decrement in the concentration for Cr(VI) was used to monitor the changes from Cr(VI) to Cr(III). In acidic conditions (pH2), Cr(VI) oxidizes DPC to 1,5-diphenylcarbazone (DPCA) resulting in a purple colored Cr(III)-DPCA complex (λ max = 540 nm) [24]. First, dried K2Cr2O7 (100 °C, 2 h) was dissolved in ultra-pure water in a concentration of 500 ppm, then diluted to 50 ppm. A second dissolution of DPC (250 mg) in ethanol (50 mL) was sonicated 30 min. H2SO4 10 % (v/v) was prepared. Reference contains Cr(VI) 50 ppm and sample is a solution of CDs (1:119 of CDs solution at concentration of 0.1 mg/mL in 50 ppm of Cr(VI)). Both were measured at the onset of the experiment and then after being under sunlight. The absorbance spectra were measured from 4 mL containing 200 μL of sample or reference, 2.8 mL of ultra-pure water (pH regulated to 2 using H2SO4 10 % (v/v)) with the addition of 1 mL of DPC solution [25].

The fluorescence spectra were measured in a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer. The absorbance was recorded from a Thermo Fisher scientific Evo-60 spectrophotometer. The FTIR spectra were obtained in a Varian 660-IR spectrophotometer. XPS/UPS/SXI-SEM, type Scanning XPS Microprobe, model PHI 5000 VersaProbe II was used for XPS tests. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were recorded on Malvern Nano Zetasizer nano series model-ZEN 3600.

Optical characteristics of the as prepared CDs are included in Fig. 2 a. CDs absorbance indicate three absorbance bands: at 240 nm attributed to π→π* transitions within the carbonized cores, at 300 nm and a prominent and wide band at 550 nm from the n→π* transition of conjugated CO and CN bonds indicating the existence of surface states [26]. The CDs exhibit emission in the wide range of the visible spectra with three main excitation and emission wavelengths at 360 nm (ex.)/431 nm (em.), 430 nm (ex.)/501 nm (em.) and 550 nm (ex.)/622 nm (em.). The excitation wavelength dependent PL emission of the CDs (Fig. 2b) is attributed to different sized particles and surface defects [27]. The relative QY was determined to be
∼ 40 %
(Fig. S1).

FTIR spectrum of the CDs with respect to the precursor/dopant is shown in Fig. 2c. Typical spectrum corresponding to urea shows two peaks at 3422 and 3333 cm− 1 (asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the NH– bond), at 1590 cm− 1 (deformation vibrations of the N–H) and at 1458 cm− 1 (stretching vibrations of the CN– bonds). Similarly, typical FTIR spectra of citric acid exhibit a broad peak of O–H stretching (R–C(O)–OH) at around 3300 cm-1, a vibration of CO stretching (C(O)–OH) at 1718 cm−1, the band of C–OH stretching at 1110 cm−1, and a peak of CH rocking at 780 cm−1. Following the treatment with formic acid, a broad band around 3660-2240 cm−1 corresponding to the hydrophilic amine (N–H) and hydroxyl (O–H) groups were exhibited which guarantees high solubility in polar solvents. The bands at 1648 cm−1, 1547 cm−1, and 1348 cm−1 are attributed to CN/CO, CC, and C–N stretching, respectively [28]. The estimated particle size was determined to be 30 ± 5 nm by DLS.

As-prepared CDs are in solid state and hence enable high chemical stability during storage under ambient conditions for long duration of time. In addition, the effect of NaCl concentration, pH and suspension time in aqueous solution, on optical response of CDs has also been investigated (Fig. S2).

The PL and detection characteristics of CDs for different metal ions exhibit wavelength-dependent variations. Fig. 2 illustrates the photoluminescence emission spectra of CDs in an aqueous solution (reference) and spiked with metal ions when excited with 360 nm (Figs. 2d), 430 nm (Figs. 2e) and 550 nm (Fig. 2f) wavelengths. At 360 nm of excitation the spectra exhibit two distinct peaks in PL emission, with centers at around 431 nm and 625 nm. The presence of Cr(VI) completely suppresses (turn-off) the emission peak (red), while the emission intensity of Al(III) (purple) is increased/turned-on at a wavelength of 441 nm. The addition of Fe(III) caused a reduction/suppression in fluorescence intensity across the visible spectrum (400 nm–700 nm), specifically at wavelengths 425 nm, 506 nm, and 625 nm (brown). The addition of Fe(III) resulted in a significant increase in peak intensity (excitation of 430 nm) showing a turn-on feature, with a single peak centered at 512, in contrast to the response observed when excited with 360 nm. On the contrary, the Al(III) exhibits a reduction in intensity and a significant shift in the peak position (from 501nm of the reference) to 516 nm. Furthermore, the presence of Cr(VI) exhibits a small peak intensity centered at 540 nm, confirming its turn-off behavior potential as a specific sensor. When excited at 550 nm, the emission wavelength of pure CDs is at 622 nm with the increase in overall intensity with respect to all the tested metal ions. Additionally, all three metal ions only exhibit quenching (turn-off), unlike at the excitation wavelengths of 360 and 431 nm in which Al (III) was turned-on, similar to turn-on phenomenon revealed at excitation wavelength 431 nm for Fe(III).

The influence of different anions (Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−) on the PL of CDs was explored at three different excitation wavelengths for copper, nickel, cadmium, and silver salts (Fig. S3). The designed multimodal probe could serve as an optical sensor for the identification of several metal ions as well as anions.

The changes in PL emission as a function of time (Fig. 3 a–c) (up to 900 min after mixing CDs and Cr(VI) (concentration 2.5 mM)), at λex = 360 nm (Fig. 3a), λem max ∼503 nm, reveal a slight increase in PL intensity, accompanied by a red shift of ∼15 nm, from commencement to 60 min. After 60 min, the intensity of PL bands starts decreasing (Fig. 3a). When the solution of CDs/Cr(VI) is excited at 430 nm, the emission band is displaced ∼15 nm to the red in comparison to the reference and the PL intensity is drastically increased after the first 20 s. The increase in PL intensity at low excitation energy could be related with the avoided aggregation of CDs leading to the hole-electron recombination during the Cr(VI) reduction process [29–31]. The emission band is continuously turned on until 304 min and moved ∼7 nm more to the red. The PL turn-off and ∼6 nm of additional red shift is observed after 900 min. In contrast, at an excitation wavelength of 550 nm, the CDs/Cr(VI) emits at 625 nm (initial time) and the intensity of the emission band is highly reduced after 2 min. After 900 min the emission is almost extinct. The PL variations could be related to a reduction reaction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) by the CDs. At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm the effect is less notorious which allows to use the CDs as optical sensor of Cr(VI) in a range of
0.01 μ M − 5 m M
after 2 min of contact time with response in two bands (Fig. 3d). The limits of detection (LOD) estimated by
3 σ / m
are
1.3
μ M
and
0.81
μ M
for both emission bands at 431 and 616 nm, respectively (Fig. 3e).

The Cr(VI) reduction to the relative non-toxic Cr(III) due to the CDs was supported by the DPC colorimetric test (Fig. 3f). XPS wide-scan spectrum for CDs with Cr(VI) (Fig. S4a) displays the signals of C 1s (283.91eV), O1s (529.37eV), N 1s (398.21eV) and Cr 2p (575.74 eV). The relative content ratio of C/O/N/Cr/K is 70.1 %/21.7 %/2.9 %/2.8 %/2.5 %. The XPS high-resolution (HR) spectrum of Cr 2p (Fig. 4 a) evidence the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). The deconvoluted peaks of Cr(VI) are displayed at 578.26 eV (Cr 2p 3/2) and 586.95 eV (Cr 2p 1/2) while the corresponding bands to Cr(III) are at 575.65eV (Cr 2p 3/2) and 584.72 eV (Cr 2p 1/2) [32,33]. Cr(III) represents ∼80 % in the mix of Cr(VI)/Cr(III). Fig. 4b shows the suggested chemical reactions occurring in the photoreduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), activated by the exciton formation in CDs [25,34,35]. XPS HR spectra of C 1s, O 1s and N 1s are included in Figs. S4b–d. HR spectrum of C 1s is resolved in four bands at 284.30 eV, due to CC, 284.60 eV, assigned to C–C bond, 292.60eV referred to K2p3/2 and 295.39 eV to K2p1/2 [36,37]. O 1s HR spectrum displays three sub-peaks. The signal at 527.20 eV could be attributed to the lattice oxygen, the band at 529.96 eV, is originated for a metal oxide and 532.29 eV could be produced by C–O/CO/OH bonds [38,39]. The HR N 1s spectrum shows two peaks of 398.5 eV due to the C–N–C and 399.51 eV attributed to the N–H bonds [40].

The ability of Cr(VI) and Fe(III) to quench the PL of CDs has been attributed to the electron transfer process. Cr(VI) leads to a non-radiative recombination of the electron-hole pair by electron transfer which is owing to the presence of vacant d orbital and low-lying d-d transition states [41]. In addition, a good spectral overlap between the excitation and emission band (excitation at 360 nm/emission 431 nm) of the CDs with the three absorption bands (centered at 260, 360, and 440 nm) of the chromium, suggests the possibility of fluorescence quenching aiding the inner filter effect (IFE) [42]. Fe(III) possess five half-filled d orbitals in the outer shell, when it is in contact with CDs, the electrons in the conduction band can fill the 3d orbital of Fe(III) generating the PL decrease. However, the static quenching mechanism (formation of non-radiative complexes Fe(III)/CDs) is the most frequently mechanism proposed [43].

The schematic representation and the actually observed diffraction of light by the polyNIPAM matrix are shown in Fig. 5 and b. The dispersed composite on the silicon substrate is illuminated with white light (Fig. 5b). The average diameter of the polyNIPAM spheres is 650 nm and their separation is approx. 430 nm, as observed in the top view of the scanning electron micrograph (Fig. 5c).

The emission spectra of the photonic structure were obtained by exciting the composite with a wavelength of 254 nm (Fig. 6 ), no significant photoluminescence emissions were detected when excited at lower energy. The photonic structure exhibited good sensitivity in detecting metal ions, specifically Cr(VI) and Fe(III). Increasing the concentration of metal ions leads to a decrease in peak intensities at 300, 352, and 592 nm. Fe(III) exhibits greater sensitivity than Cr(VI) at identical concentrations.

A comparison of the versatile applications of the synthesized CDs with other reported CDs is depicted in Table 1 . In addition, this ratiometric probe can eliminate interference limitations in recognizing heavy metal ions. PL characteristics of CDs solution and CDs/PVA/polyNIPAM microsphere are summarized in Table S1.

CDs with three main emission bands at wavelengths of 425 nm, 496 nm, and 612 nm, corresponding to excitation wavelengths of 360 nm, 430 nm, and 550 nm, respectively, were used to selectively identify Fe(III), Al(III) and Cr(VI) through their characteristic features in ratiometric approach. With complete quenching from Cr(VI), the PL intensity is turned on at 430 nm excitation, turned-off with 330 nm excitation, accompanied with a redshift of the band, facilitating the specific detection of Fe(III) ions. The presence of Al(III) was detected by observing both turn-on (360 and 430 nm) along with a redshift and turn-off PL signals, at an excitation wavelength of 550 nm. The CDs induced reduction of Cr(VI) to the less harmful Cr(III), was monitored by PL at the three different excitation wavelengths and confirmed through colorimetric method and XPS analysis. Additionally, CDs served as optical sensors of chromium at excitation 360 nm. The study also involved formation of CDs/PVA/PolyNIPAM composite 2D luminescent photonic structures for detecting Fe(III) and Cr(VI) in anticounterfeiting applications.

M. Rangel-Ayala: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. V. Castillo-Gallardo: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Y. Kumar: Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing J.S. Pérez-Huerta: Supervision. Claudia Pacholski: Resources. V. Agarwal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: MR (592668) and VCG (636541) acknowledge CONAHCYT for the postdoctoral scholarship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.116071.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heavy metal pollution in the aquatic environment: efficient and low-cost removal approaches to eliminate their toxicity: a review
RSC Adv.

Rahman, Heavy metal pollution in the aquatic environment: efficient and low-cost removal approaches to eliminate their toxicity: a review, RSC Adv. 13 (2023) 17595-17610. https://doi.org/10.1039/D3RA00723E.

iScience

Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res.

Clin. Chim. Acta
